# Chapter 24

[3k9m1a7d] Weeks later the desert was still there, unmoved. The news loops had moved on. The clips of fire and metal and men screaming had been chopped into ads for energy drinks and “authentic experience” resorts. Flynn watched one in a diner outside of a pod-stop and felt his stomach turn at how clean the sound was after they’d mixed it. Outside, the heat shimmered off the blacktop like a lie.

[f0w6n2q8] He didn’t go back to the Compound. He drove past the turnoff once, at dusk, and saw the dark hump of the shipping containers against the sky. No lights. No laughter. Just geometry and dust. It might have been abandoned or it might have been waiting, but either way it didn’t feel like home. He kept going.

[9t2c5x0r] Don Castellanos had let him keep the Charger. Not as a gift exactly. More like an afterthought. At the compound, after the finish, men had tried to put a drink in his hand and tell him he’d done something noble. Don had watched him with those patient eyes, then nodded once toward the keys. Take it. Go. Flynn took the keys because it was easier than refusing.

[h7v1p4s6] The Charger was loud in a way the pods weren’t allowed to be. Even idling it sounded like it wanted to bite. He liked that. He liked that it demanded something from him—attention, hands on the wheel, a body in the seat. He didn’t race it. He just drove it in the mornings when the air was cooler and the horizon looked almost merciful.

[2n8d6k3y] He rented a room behind a tire shop in a town that used to have a name people said with pride. Now it was a place you passed through on the way to somewhere sealed and air-conditioned. The shop owner didn’t ask questions. He’d seen the Charger and understood the kind of person who would still choose a car over a pod. He took cash and left Flynn alone.

[m4q0j7b2] At night Flynn lay on the mattress and listened to the building creak as it cooled. In the dark he saw the Shelby turning. He saw Birdie’s hands on the seam of the door, pulling until skin left him. He heard Cage laugh, then cough, then call his name like it was a curse. He would wake with sweat on his ribs and the taste of smoke in his mouth.

[x1c8v5h9] Diesel was two states away in a hospital that smelled like bleach and soft plastic. The building had screens on every wall telling you how safe you were. Flynn found her by asking questions in places where questions weren’t welcome and paying a nurse who pretended not to notice. Diesel lay in a bed with her leg in a cast and her cheek bruised yellow-green.

[5r2m9e0k] She looked smaller without grease on her hands. Still hard, though. Her eyes tracked him as he came in like she was measuring whether he was real. “You’re alive,” she said. It wasn’t warmth. It was inventory. Flynn nodded. He sat in the chair by her bed and watched the monitor climb and fall with her breath.

[c6a3p1z7] “Birdie?” she asked. Flynn told her where he was. Different hospital. Burns and breaks and a mind that didn’t fit right inside his skull anymore. Diesel closed her eyes. For a long time she didn’t speak. When she did her voice was flat. “Cage?” Flynn said, “Dead,” and watched her face for anything like relief.

[q9h5d2n1] She didn’t smile. She didn’t cry. She just breathed out, slow. “He should’ve died a long time ago,” she said. Then she turned her head on the pillow and looked at the white ceiling like it offended her. “You did it?” Flynn said nothing. Diesel’s mouth tightened. “Doesn’t matter,” she said. “He would’ve taken someone else with him if you didn’t.”

[7s0f8v3t] A screen above the bed tried to sell Diesel a vacation package while her pain meds dripped. She reached up with her good hand and slapped it dark. Flynn watched her and thought about how the world never stopped talking. “What now?” he asked. Diesel’s eyes slid back to him. “Now I heal,” she said. “Then I get out. Somewhere green. Somewhere it rains. Somewhere nobody knows his name.”

[w3k6p9u4] “You think that exists?” Flynn asked. Diesel shrugged as much as her ribs would let her. “Maybe not,” she said. “But I want to try. You should too.” Flynn looked at his hands. The knuckles were still stained with old grease that wouldn’t come out. “I don’t know what I am without it,” he said.

[e8n1g4c0] Diesel’s gaze held him. “You’re a kid,” she said. The word hit him wrong. He wasn’t a kid in the way Birdie was. He wasn’t a man in the way Cage pretended to be. He was something in between, stretched thin by speed. Diesel’s voice softened a fraction. “Don’t turn your fear into a religion. That’s what he did.”

[0d7x2m5p] He visited Wrench in a facility that had gardens on the roof for people who forgot what dirt felt like. Wrench sat in a chair by a window that showed a fake pond with plastic reeds. His arms had healed wrong. His hands trembled like engines out of tune. When Flynn said his name, Wrench blinked twice and stared through him as if Flynn was a reflection.

[1v4q8s2h] Flynn sat beside him and didn’t try to make it better with words. Wrench’s mouth worked sometimes, silent. Once he whispered, “He coming?” and Flynn’s throat tightened. “No,” Flynn said. “He’s not coming.” Wrench nodded like he was hearing it for the first time, then turned his head back toward the window.

[t6b3k0y9] Victor Khoury’s face showed up on a feed while Flynn waited in a clinic lobby. The billionaire’s hair had gone gray at the temples in a month. He spoke about trauma like it was a technical problem and the camera loved him for it. Lawsuit. Security failures. New protocols. When the interviewer asked about the cars, Khoury’s eyes flicked away. “They were just objects,” he said, and Flynn could hear the lie rattling.

[8j2p6a1n] The guard from the museum had quit and moved into an arcology job where he never had to touch anyone. Another feed called it a “recovery journey.” Flynn remembered the guard’s face in the garage light, the sound of his head hitting the concrete, and felt sick at the word journey. People survived. The machines brought them back. The rest of them stayed broken in invisible places.

[r0m7d3c8] Flynn drove the Charger out beyond the solar farms where the panels ended and the land went rough again. The road cracked. The shoulder became sand. He shut the engine off and the silence came in like water. In the distance a dust devil walked across the flats, turning slow, like it had all day.

[p5c1v9w3] He built a small fire in a wash where the wind was low. Not for warmth. For light. For the old feeling of sitting where a screen couldn’t find you. He watched the flame eat dry mesquite and thought about Cage’s face at two hundred kilometers per hour, grinning like the world owed him the impact.

[6y8h0k4s] He didn’t want money from Don. Didn’t want a red ’Cuda and a case of cash that would draw hungry eyes. He wanted the simple thing: the car, the noise, the control. But even that felt poisoned. When he touched the steering wheel sometimes he felt like he was touching Cage’s hand through time.

[z2e5m7q0] He went to see Birdie on a day the nurses said he was “having a good window.” Birdie’s burns were bandaged. His leg was in a brace. His hair had been shaved where they’d stitched his scalp. He looked too young again, stripped of the swagger. When Flynn walked in Birdie’s eyes widened like he expected to be hit.

[b9n0t6x1] “You brought him back?” Birdie asked. His voice was small and precise, like he’d learned to speak carefully. Flynn sat in the chair and said, “No.” Birdie swallowed. “You left him,” he said. It wasn’t accusation. It was trying to understand how the universe worked.

[4c7p2r8v] Flynn said, “I couldn’t get him out.” Birdie watched him, then nodded once, too quick. He wanted to believe that. Flynn let him have it because the other truth was heavier. Birdie’s fingers worried the edge of his blanket. “Was he always like that?” he asked, eyes on the sheet.

[n6s1k5d9] Flynn stared at the wall where a cartoon sun explained pain management. “I think so,” he said. “We just liked the story where he wasn’t.” Birdie’s mouth twisted. For a moment he looked like he might laugh, then he cried instead, silent tears that ran into the bandage tape. Flynn sat with him and let the silence do what it could.

[y8w4m2a6] “He said I was his brother,” Birdie whispered. “He said he’d never leave me.” Flynn felt anger rise and had nowhere to put it. “He wasn’t anyone’s brother,” Flynn said. Birdie flinched at the harshness. Flynn lowered his voice. “He was a storm. You don’t make promises to storms.”

[3u9c6h0e] Birdie looked up then, eyes wet but sharp. “You’re not going to leave me,” he said, not as a question. Flynn hesitated. He didn’t want to lie. He also didn’t want to abandon another kid to the clean institutional world that would smooth his edges and sell him therapy plans. Flynn said, “I’ll come back,” and meant it as best as he could.

[k0m5p8s3] Outside the hospital a pod curbside line moved with perfect calm. People stepped in and out like parcels. Flynn stood by the Charger and watched them and felt like he was looking at a different species. Everyone safe. Everyone alive. Everyone drifting. He wondered if Cage had been right about one thing: that safety could become a kind of death.

[7g2n9r4x] But then he thought of Diesel in the helicopter, teeth clenched against pain, and the thought curdled. Safety wasn’t the enemy. Cage was. Cage had taken the hunger for realness and turned it into an excuse to hurt people. Flynn could feel the same hunger in himself, still alive, still wanting speed and risk and the clean moment where consequences were immediate.

[a1d8y6t2] One evening he drove out past the town until the road turned to dirt and the dirt turned to track. The Charger bounced and complained. He killed the engine in a shallow basin and listened to it tick as it cooled. The stars came out, hard and cold, like nails in black wood.

[j4q7m1v8] He built another small fire. He wasn’t sure why he kept doing it. Habit. Ceremony. The need to put a circle of light around himself so the dark felt less absolute. He sat with his back to the Charger and watched the flame shrink and swell with the wind.

[9k3x0n6p] Footsteps came from the dark. Not heavy. Not careful either. Flynn’s hand went to the knife in his pocket before his mind decided. A kid stepped into the firelight. Sixteen maybe. Sunburned nose. Cheap sneakers. He held his hands out to show he didn’t have a weapon, the way people had learned to perform harmlessness.

[f6n2c8w0] The kid looked at the Charger like it was a holy thing. “That yours?” he asked. His voice had that same tremor Flynn remembered in his own, a mix of fear and appetite. Flynn didn’t answer right away. He studied the kid’s face and saw curiosity hungry enough to become anything.

[2h5s9m3r] “Yeah,” Flynn said finally. The kid nodded fast. “I seen you on the road. Thought it was… I don’t know. I thought cars like that were dead.” Flynn stared into the fire and thought about how many men had died proving they weren’t. “Most are,” he said.

[v7a1p4k9] The kid took a cautious step closer. “How do you drive it?” he asked. “I mean—pods don’t let you—” He trailed off like the words might trigger an alarm. Flynn felt the edge of a smile that wasn’t pleasant. “With your hands,” he said. “And your mistakes.”

[m8c0d7y5] The kid sat on his heels at the edge of the light. He watched Flynn the way Birdie used to watch Cage, but without the madness. Just hunger. Flynn could tell him to go home. He could tell him about Wrench’s scream and Diesel’s blood and Birdie’s burns. He could say run. He could break the chain right here.

[x9t3b0n7] Flynn stared at the flame until it blurred. In the dark beyond it the Charger sat waiting, a shape of metal and promise. He heard Cage’s voice in his head, saying you’re just like me, and felt something cold in his chest. He looked at the kid and saw himself a year ago, empty and ready to be filled.

[0p6k2s8c] “Sit down,” Flynn said. The kid sat. Flynn pointed at the Charger’s hood line, at the way the body sat over the wheels, at the stance. He began talking about engines. About fuel. About heat. About what it meant to listen to a machine and hear what it needed. The kid leaned forward, eyes bright.

[w1n7m5q4] Flynn didn’t tell him everything. Not yet. He told him enough to open the door. Enough to let the hunger have a name. The fire burned low. The desert listened the way it always did, indifferent. Somewhere far away a pod hummed down a road with its passenger asleep. In the basin the Charger waited, and beside it the cycle found a new place to root.
